paper_title: PokeRL: Reinforcement Learning for Pokemon Red

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Pokemon Red is a long-horizon JRPG with sparse rewards, partial observability, and quirky control mechanics that make it a challenging benchmark for reinforcement learning. While recent work has shown that PPO agents can clear the first two gyms using heavy reward shaping and engineered observations, training remains brittle in practice, with agents often degenerating into action loops, menu spam, or unproductive wandering. In this paper, we present PokeRL, a modular system that trains deep reinforcement learning agents to complete early game tasks in Pokemon Red, including exiting the player's house, exploring Pallet Town to reach tall grass, and winning the first rival battle. Our main contributions are a loop-aware environment wrapper around the PyBoy emulator with map masking, a multi-layer anti-loop and anti-spam mechanism, and a dense hierarchical reward design. We argue that practical systems like PokeRL, which explicitly model failure modes such as loops and spam, are a necessary intermediate step between toy benchmarks and full Pokemon League champion agents. Code is available at https://github.com/reddheeraj/PokemonRL

sections.title: Start
sections.content: PokeRL: Reinforcement Learning for Pokemon Red Dheeraj Reddy Mudireddy Texas A&M Institute of Data Science Texas A&M University College Station, USA dheeraj.reddy@tamu.edu Sai Patibandla Dept. of Computer Science Texas A&M University College Station, USA patibas@tamu.edu 6 2 0 2 2 1 ] . [ 1 2 1 8 0 1 . 4 0 6 2 : r AbstractPokemon Red is long-horizon JRPG with sparse rewards, partial observability, and quirky control mechanics that make it challenging benchmark for reinforcement learning. While recent work has shown that PPO agents can clear the first two gyms using heavy reward shaping and engineered observations, training remains brittle in practice, with agents often degenerating into action loops, menu spam, or unproductive wandering. In this paper we present PokeRL, modular system that trains deep reinforcement learning agents to complete earlygame tasks in Pokemon Red, including exiting the players house, exploring Pallet Town to reach tall grass, and winning the first rival battle. Our main contributions are loop-aware environment wrapper around the PyBoy emulator with map masking, multi-layer anti-loop and anti-spam mechanism, and dense hierarchical reward design. We argue that practical systems like PokeRL, which explicitly model failure modes such as loops and spam, are necessary intermediate step between toy benchmarks and full Pokemon League champion agents. Code is available at https://github.com/reddheeraj/PokemonRL. I. INTRODUCTION Classic game environments have been central to the progress of deep reinforcement learning, from Atari and Go to NetHack and Minecraft. These domains combine high-dimensional observations with long time horizons and have repeatedly revealed the limitations of standard RL algorithms in the presence of sparse rewards and deceptive local optima. Pokemon Red occupies particularly interesting point in this landscape. Unlike reflex-oriented arcade games, it is story-driven JRPG where progress depends on coordinated navigation, interaction, and turn-based combat, executed over tens of thousands of timesteps. The player must leave their house, traverse Pallet Town and nearby routes, collect starter Pokemon, engage in wild battles to gain experience, and defeat scripted opponents. From an RL perspective, this yields: Extreme rewards assignment: Key rewards such as catching Pokemon or winning battle may occur only after long sequences of actions. High branching factor: Even in the opening minutes there are many movement options and interaction opportunities. Partial observability: The agent sees only the current 2D screen, while crucial variables (HP, map ID, party status) live in hidden memory. Non-standard controls: Movement requires double press (turn then step), and multiple menu buttons exist that are useless or harmful for learning. Recent work has shown that PPO agents can complete roughly the first fifth of Pokemon Red by using custom Gym environment, dense reward shaping, and an additional visited mask observation channel [6]. However, reproducing these results is non-trivial. In our own attempts, we observed several recurring pathologies: agents exploiting shaping rewards by looping, spamming the button or the no-op action, spinning in place because the environment mishandled double-press movement, and wandering within small region without meaningful exploration. PokeRL is our attempt to build more robust and transparent environment for early-game Pokemon Red. Instead of only tuning rewards, we explicitly encode loop detection, spam penalties, and spatial memory into the environment. We then structure training as curriculum over three sequences: exiting the house, reaching tall grass from Pallet Town, and winning the first rival battle. The result is not full game-solving agent, but working system that reliably acquires non-trivial behaviors and surfaces many of the design trade-offs involved in turning RL + emulator into practical research platform. II. MOTIVATION AND PROBLEM SETTING A. Why Pokemon Red? Pokemon Red is natural testbed for long-horizon RL because it embeds several sub-tasks commonly studied in isolation: Navigation and exploration in 2D grid-like overworld. Inventory and resource management through items and Pokemon party composition. Turn-based combat with discrete actions, stochastic outcomes, and non-trivial strategy as shown in Figure 1c. The opening segment of the game provides compact yet challenging subset of these skills: 1) House exit: Learn basic movement and door interaction to leave Reds bedroom and house. 2) Early exploration: Navigate Pallet Town and Route 1 to reach tall grass and trigger scripted encounters. 3) First rival battle: Execute viable battle actions to defeat the rivals Pokemon given fixed starter. Our project focuses on these three tasks because they are short enough to allow experimentation on single device and rich enough to expose the core issues of loop, sparse rewards, and bad exploration. (a) Red in his house (b) Exploring Pallet Town (c) First Battle Fig. 1: Frames of selected objectives in the game. B. Challenges in Vanilla RL Setup Preliminary experiments with simple PPO agent wrapped around PyBoy highlighted five concrete failure modes: Infinite action loops: The agent discovered local cycles (for example, pacing between two tiles) that gave small positive movement rewards without progress. Reward sparsity and instability: When only rare events were rewarded, episode returns fluctuated wildly, and policies failed to improve. Button spam: Action distributions collapsed onto A, Start, or no-op, producing pointless menus or idling. Incorrect movement semantics: Treating single direction press as move one tile caused the agent to spin in place instead of walking. Exploration without memory: Without an explicit notion of visited states the agent repeatedly revisited already explored areas. PokeRL is designed as an answer to these five issues. III. RELATED WORK Deep RL in video games began with agents that achieved human-level performance on Atari games by learning directly from pixels with score-based rewards. Games such as Montezumas Revenge exposed the limitations of this approach: rewards were extremely sparse, and naive exploration strategies failed to reach them. Work on intrinsic motivation and Go-Explore [2] addressed this by explicitly rewarding novelty and maintaining state memories to revisit promising situations. Until recently, little academic literature existed on applying RL to Pokemon. For Pokemon specifically, Pleines et al [5] introduced the first comprehensive deep RL agent for Pokemon Red, using PPO [9] over PyBoy-based Gym environment. They engineered reward function with more than 25 components and augmented the observation with 7280 visited mask channel, enabling an agent to progress up to the second gym in Cerulean City, beating two major gym leaders in pokemon battles. This was milestone achievement: as of February 2025, an RL policy (with <10 million parameters) could beat Pokemon Reds early game to an extent with only few simplifications to the game logic. They showed that certain reward bonuses could backfire, as agents learned to exploit them in unintended ways. Their ablation studies showed both the necessity and risk of dense shaping: certain rewards encouraged grinding or skipping mandatory events when tuned poorly. Parallel community projects have applied RL to other Pokemon titles, such as Pokemon Gold, and constructed tools like poke-env [7], which interfaces RL algorithms with the Pokemon Showdown battle simulator. These battle-only settings provide cleaner reward signals (win or loss) and smaller action spaces, and have been pushed to near humanparity using offline RL and large language model policies. For example, PokeLLMon (2024) [3] integrated large language model as the decision-making policy and reached humanparity in Pokemon Showdown battles by leveraging prior knowledge of move effectiveness and strategy. These battlefocused studies, while not addressing the exploration aspect, contribute insights on reward design (win/loss or HP-based rewards) and action masking (disabling illegal moves) that ensure the RL agent learns valid battle strategies. More recently, the NeurIPS 2025 PokeAgent Challenge [4] proposed tracks for Pokemon Emerald speedrunning and competitive play, positioning Pokemon as benchmark for sequential decision making. Adding onto the community projects, in 2023, YouTube video demonstrating an RL agent playing Pokemon went viral (7.5+ million views) [10]. That project was built on PyBoy and OpenAI Gym as well, and it inspired many subsequent experiments. Bloggers and engineers have tried related ideas on other Pokemon titles; for instance, Bas de Haan documented project training an agent on Pokemon Gold version [1]. In his write-up, he emphasizes the importance of reading emulator memory for key game variables (to compute custom rewards for things like experience points or map exploration). Our work is closest in spirit to the open PokeRL framework and its academic counterpart, but focuses on explicitly engineered anti-loop and anti-spam mechanisms, per-map visited mask with centered origin, and curriculum over early-game sequences rather than full game completion. IV. SYSTEM OVERVIEW PokeRL is built as custom Gymnasium environment around PyBoy with additional modules for memory reading, reward computation, and curriculum control. A. Actor-Critic Network The agents policy and value functions are implemented using the Stable-Baselines3 CnnPolicy, which provides compact yet expressive convolutional actorcritic model well suited for visual environments. As displayed in Figure 2, the network processes an 8-channel input composed of four consecutive grayscale game frames and their corresponding visited-mask frames, to capture both short-term temporal dynamics and spatial exploration history. Three convolutional layers progressively downsample the 7280 input while expanding feature depth from 8 to 64 channels, producing 1,920-dimensional latent encoding. This representation is then passed through fully connected layer with 512 units, forming the shared backbone for both the policy and value heads. The policy head outputs logits over the seven allowed actions (movement, A, B, and no-op), while the value head predicts the scalar state value. The model contains 1.07 million trainable parameters, making it lightweight enough for fast iteration while still capable of learning structured behaviors. B. Observation Space The base observation is downsampled grayscale image of the Game Boy screen (7280) as shown in Figure 1b. To give the agent short-term temporal context and long-term spatial memory, we augment this as follows: 1) Frame stacking: Four consecutive frames are concatenated along the channel dimension. 2) Per-map visited mask: For each map we maintain 7280 binary mask that marks tiles the agent has already visited. The mask is centered at the entry position of that map, so as the camera scrolls, the mask remains aligned with global map coordinates. This is illustrated in Figure 3. The final observation has shape 72802 before stacking and 72808 after stacking, which is then transposed to channels-first for CNN policy. C. Action Space and Double-Press Handling We restrict the agent to seven actions: up, down, left, right, A, B, and no-op. Start and Select are removed because they open menu and are prime target for spam. At one point, the agent learned to save the game after coming to the ground floor in his house. Pokemon Red uses double-press mechanic where the first directional press rotates the character and only the second moves the player one tile. We implement this directly in the environment: any movement action issues two press-release cycles to PyBoy. This abstraction ensures that single RL action corresponds to an intuitive step in the grid world. D. Memory Reader To define precise rewards and termination conditions, we read selected addresses from the games RAM via PyBoy. The RAM addresses are mentioned in Table I. This information is not passed directly to the policy but is used by the environment to compute events such as entered tall grass or battle started. TABLE I: Direct Memory Reading Addresses Data Player Position (Y) Player Position (X) Map ID Party Count Battle State Pokemon HP Memory Address 0xD361 0xD362 0xD35E 0xD163 0xD057 0xD16C V. REWARD DESIGN AND CURRICULUM A. Hierarchical Rewards We design dense reward function with three conceptual levels: micro, meso, and macro. Representative terms are shown in the Table II. To counterbalance these positives, we add small penalties for staying in place, repeated button presses, and detected loops (section VI). After several iterations, we settled on relatively mild negative values in the range 0.02 to 0.2 to avoid overwhelming positive rewards. TABLE II: Reward Structure for Agent in Environment Reward Type Reward Description Micro Rewards (per step) Movement to new tile Euclidean distance moved Visiting position for the first time +1.0 +0.2 distance +0.5 Meso Rewards (sub-goals) Transitioning to new map Entering map for the first time in an episode Exploration (large unexplored region reached) Macro Rewards (rare events) Entering tall grass Starting battle Catching Pokemon / winning Sequence 3 battle +10.0 +5.0 +2.0 +20.0 +10.0 +50.0 B. Curriculum over Three Sequences Instead of training single policy on the full game start, we split the problem into three sequences, each with its own configuration and save state: 1) Sequence 1: House Exit Start in Reds upstairs bedroom. Episode ends when the map ID changes to indicate outdoors or when step limit is exceeded. Fig. 2: The Actor-Critic Network tile exceeds three visits the agent receives small negative reward, and larger penalty beyond five visits. 2) Action pattern detection: Using sliding window over 20 recent actions, we check for repetitive patterns such as alternating and or fixed two-action cycles. Repeated detection adds negative reward, while breaking out of such patterns yields small bonus. 3) Position loop detection: We track the last several positions and detect when the agent repeatedly returns within small radius of previous location. This is treated as loop and penalized. Fig. 4: Effect of anti-loop system on training episodes This system directly encodes the notion that doing the same thing in the same place is unproductive. We instrumented the environment to log the position visits and action patterns during training. loop episode is any episode where single position was visited >10 times OR action pattern detection triggered >20 times. We then analyzed 1000 Fig. 3: downsampled frame and its visited mask. Rewards emphasize movement and the first map transitions. 2) Sequence 2: Exploration to Grass Start outside Reds front door. Episode ends when the agent reaches tall grass, triggers Professor Oaks scripted event, or times out. Rewards emphasize exploration coverage and reaching the grass region. 3) Sequence 3: First Rival Battle Start at the beginning of the rival battle with fixed starter Pokemon. Rewards focus on using offensive moves, fainting the opponents Pokemon, and winning the battle. This curriculum mirrors the games natural progression and allows each skill to be learned under simpler reward structure. VI. LOOP AND SPAM MITIGATION A. Anti-Loop System To combat infinite action loops we introduce three-layer anti-loop mechanism: 1) Position visit penalties: We maintain dictionary of counts for each (x, y) position within an episode. When using DummyVecEnv, with one instance optionally rendered for video inspection. For each sequence and configuration, we ran multiple training sessions for several hundred thousand timesteps on single CPU & GPU. Metrics are logged to TensorBoard and additionally extracted into analysis scripts that produced figures. VIII. RESULTS A. Effect of Anti-Spam on Action Distribution Before any spam controls, action counts showed strong collapse onto and no-op: roughly 32.1% presses and 28.4% no-ops, with only 27.2% of actions being actual movement. After applying graduated penalties and removing Start/Select, movement actions rose to 68.2% of all actions, A/B combined fell to 24.3%, and no-op dropped to 7.5%. Shannon entropy (equation 1) of the action distribution increased from 1.21 bits (out of 3.17 bit maximum for nine actions) to 1.82 bits (out of 2.81 for seven actions), corresponding to about an 50% relative improvement in exploration efficiency. These results are visualized in Figure 5, and they align qualitatively with the goal of promoting movement over idling or spamming. B. Exploration with Per-Map Visited Mask We ran an ablation study comparing two variants of the environment for the exploration sequence (2nd sequence): No mask: Observations are grayscale frames only. Visited mask: Observations include the additional permap visited channel. We conducted an A/B comparision over 300k timesteps for each variant. Certain metrics like unique positions (Count of distinct (x, y) coordinates visited), revisit ratio (total steps / unique positions), and exploration ratio (Percentage of reachable tiles visited in the map) were tracked per episode. The results turned out to be the following, as highlighted by Figure 6: average unique positions per episode increasing from 34.2 to 48.1 (+40.6%). exploration coverage in Pallet Town rising from 12% of tiles to 41%. Fig. 5: Actions distribution before vs after anti-spam implementation episodes before and after anti-loop implementation, drawing out the results mentioned in Figure 4. B. Anti-Spam Mechanism We observed that PPO policies quickly converged to policies that hammered the button, no-op, or menu buttons. Naively imposing large penalties for these actions caused episode returns to become highly negative and made training unstable. Our final anti-spam design uses graduated streak penalties: Streak counters for A, B, and staying in the same position. Small penalty (0.1) after three consecutive presses of the same button. Additional penalty (0.2) beyond five presses. Tiny positive reward when the last several actions include at least four distinct actions, encouraging diversity. We also entirely removed Start and Select from the action space to close the most egregious loophole. To measure the impact of this mechanism, we implemented an action counts array in the environment to track frequency of each action. Using this array, we were able to log the action distribution to tensorboard every 5000 steps during training. To statistically distinguish between before vs after anti-loop mechanism, we calculated the Shannon Entropy (H) [8], defined as: = (cid:88) p(a) log2 p(a) (1) revisit ratio decreasing from 4.8 to 3.1, which means fewer redundant revisits. where p(a) is probability of action a. Before anti-spam, = 1.21 bits while after anti-spam implementation, = 1.82 bits. This increase in indicates that the agent explores its action space more uniformly rather than collapsing to few dominant actions. VII. EXPERIMENTAL SETUP We train PPO agents using Stable-Baselines3 with the builtin CNN policy. Key hyperparameters are: learning rate 3 104, gamma = 0.999, steps = 2048, batch size = 128, and 10 epochs per update. Four parallel environments are run These differences indicate that the CNN policy effectively learned to use the mask as memory of explored areas. C. Reduction of Loop Episodes Using the loop detection criteria described earlier in section VI, we classified episodes as loop episodes if single tile was visited more than threshold number of times or if action patterns repeated too often. On 1,000-episode samples for each condition (as shown in Figure 4): Baseline (no anti-loop): 41.2% loop episodes, 58.8% normal episodes. Spatial memory as an observation channel: Providing visited mask is simple way to give the agent memory without recurrent networks. The empirical gains in exploration metrics justify this additional channel. Curriculum structure: Decomposing training into house exit, exploration, and battle sequences allowed faster iteration and clearer debugging than monolithic fullgame objective would have. At the same time, there are limitations. The reliance on direct memory access and handcrafted reward signals means our system is not fully end-to-end from pixels alone. Training remains computationally heavy, time-expensive, and sensitive to hyperparameters. X. FUTURE WORK natural next step is transitioning from independently trained sequences to unified environment where the agent must coordinate house exit, overworld navigation, and battle strategy within single long episode. Exploration quality may be further improved by augmenting the visited mask with principled intrinsic motivation methods such as curiosity rewards or Go-Explore style state archives, which could offer more reliable long-horizon discovery than deterministic loop penalties. Another promising direction is reducing manual reward shaping through learned reward models, including inverse RL or preference-based RL guided by human feedback, which may help avoid reward hacking while preserving meaningful skill acquisition. Battle performance can also be strengthened by incorporating insights from recent language-model-guided agents that demonstrate human-parity decision making in turnbased combat. Finally, packaging the full PokeRL environment as an open benchmark with standardized tasks, metrics, and baselines would enable reproducible experimentation and foster broader community-driven progress on long-horizon JRPGstyle RL challenges. XI. CONCLUSION This paper presented PokeRL, loop-aware reinforcement learning environment experiment for early-game Pokemon Red. By explicitly addressing infinite loops, reward sparsity, button spam, double-press movement, and memoryless exploration, we created system in which PPO agents learn meaningful behaviors within selected objectives: exiting the house, exploring Pallet Town, and winning the first battle. The main lesson is that successfully applying RL to rich, real games often requires as much careful system design as algorithmic innovation. Our anti-loop modules, hierarchical rewards, and visited mask are pragmatic tools that can be adapted to other long-horizon environments where agents lost, stuck, or bored. We hope this would otherwise get work contributes concrete stepping stone on the path from toy benchmarks to agents that can robustly handle the full complexity of games like Pokemon Red. Fig. 6: Exploration Metrics Comparison to see the effect of per map visited mask With anti-loop system: 4.7% loop episodes, 95.3% normal episodes. This demonstrates that the multi-layer anti-loop mechanism almost completely eliminates pathological looping behaviors while leaving enough freedom for normal exploration. D. Task-Level Performance The current project focuses on environment design and behavior shaping rather than absolute performance, but preliminary sequence-level results are promising: House exit sequence: after 150k timesteps the agent exits the house in roughly 65% of episodes. Exploration sequence: the agent reaches tall grass to in about 60% of episodes by 500k trigger an event, timesteps. Battle sequence: win rates against the rival hover around 50% after 500k timesteps with fixed reward structure. These numbers show clear learning compared to random behavior, although there is considerable headroom before approaching human consistency. There are some minor downsides as well. While running the testing scenarios, manual human trigger might be required in text dialog sequences. We believe that this can be overlooked, given the current context and intent of this project. IX. DISCUSSION PokeRL demonstrates that environment-side engineering can dramatically improve the stability and interpretability of RL training in complex games. Several design choices emerged as particularly important: Mild penalties instead of harsh ones: Large negative rewards for spam or loops initially collapsed training. Scaling them down by an order of magnitude preserved the incentive structure without drowning out positive signals. Explicit modeling of game quirks: The double-press movement mechanic is small but essential detail. Without encoding it, the agents behavior looks superficially active but achieves no positional change.

sections.title: REFERENCES
sections.content: [1] Bas de Haan. Reinforcement learning 101: Ai plays pokemon! https: //medium.com/ordina-data/reinforcement-learning-101-ai-plays-pok% C3%A9mon-e0626bd6beae, 2024. Medium blog post, May 27, 2024. [2] Adrien Ecoffet, Joost Huizinga, Joel Lehman, Kenneth O. Stanley, and Jeff Clune. Go-explore: new approach for hard-exploration problems. https://arxiv.org/abs/1901.10995, 2021. [3] Sihao Hu, Tiansheng Huang, and Ling Liu. Pokellmon: human-parity agent for pokemon battles with large language models. https://arxiv.org/ abs/2402.01118, 2024. [4] Seth Karten, Jake Grigsby, Stephanie Milani, Kiran Vodrahalli, Amy Zhang, Fei Fang, Yuke Zhu, and Chi Jin. The pokeagent challenge: Competitive and long-context learning at scale. In NeurIPS Competition Track, April 2025. [5] Marco Pleines, Daniel Addis, David Rubinstein, Frank Zimmer, Mike Preuss, and Peter Whidden. Pokemon red via reinforcement learning. https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.19920, 2025. [6] David Rubinstein. Pokemon rl observations: The visited mask. https: //drubinstein.github.io/pokerl/docs/chapter-2/observations/, 2025. [7] Haris Sahovic. Poke-env: pokemon ai in python. https://github.com/ hsahovic/poke-env. [8] Paulo Saraiva. On shannon entropy and its applications. Kuwait Journal of Science, 50(3):194199, 2023. [9] John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, and Oleg Klimov. Proximal policy optimization algorithms, 2017. [10] P. Whidden. Training ai to play pokemon with reinforcement learning. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DcYLT37ImBY, 2023.
